quote_of_day.text: If pajeet leaders spent half the energy on building factories, manufacturing, and actual R&D instead of non-stop bhangi reservation scams, caste votebank warfare and "hisab chukta" politics, India wouldn't be a laughingstock still importing everything today.
quote_of_day.name: Anon

guest_letters.title: Based website
guest_letters.body: Why aren’t we talking more about this IAC guy?? I just woke uop and checked bahut interesting site hai, there’s quotes and funny articles and board status?? We need more anons like iac to make cool shit. Im a very impressed pajeet. Writing this to see if it will get featured on the newspaper or not, im not sure how he set it up but if the !thing works then hello indus news! Im on da news niggaaaaaa
guest_letters.name: Anon

articles.title: Weather for Indus today
articles.body:
A low-pressure system of anti-reservation sentiment moved into the board this morning, bringing with it a torrent of criticism towards India's leadership and development.

The system's core message, centered around the idea that leaders should focus more on industrial and technological advancement rather than caste-based politics, drew a significant following.

A scattered high-pressure ridge of film appreciation developed in the afternoon, marked by praise for 'Animal' and its portrayal of caste issues, contrasting with criticisms of Bollywood's handling of similar themes.

This ridge brought clear skies and straightforward discussion, with one anon expressing enjoyment of the film's 'timeless' feel and notable scenes.

Meanwhile, a cold front of geopolitical tension swept through the board, marked by reports of an anon being banned from Reddit for making threatening remarks against Bangladesh and Pakistan.

This front brought choppy discussion and scattered reactions, including speculation about the caste backgrounds of fellow posters and comparisons between Sub-Saharan Africa and India. These topics introduced a layer of fog, reducing visibility and stirring debate.

articles.title: Criticism of India's Leadership and Development
articles.body:
An anonymous poster expressed frustration with India's leaders, stating they spend too much energy on scams, caste votebank warfare, and "hisab chukta" politics, rather than building factories, manufacturing, and conducting R&D.

One respondent agreed, suggesting that if India stopped certain "retardation" and focused on development, ethnic groups that excel in various fields might not abandon the nation.

Another poster calculated that pausing or redirecting funds currently spent on certain schemes could lead to significant improvements in infrastructure, such as cleaning the Yamuna river and improving sewer designs.

A discussion on India's military capabilities ensued, with one poster arguing that modernizing and expanding the military to a point of sustained offensive operations would require significant investment, estimated at around $100 billion over a decade.

Others noted that India's military manufacturing capability is not inferior, but rather underfunded, and that private partnerships could increase production.

The conversation also touched on social issues, with one poster making derogatory comments about certain castes, prompting a response that criticized the repetitive nature of such comments.

Another poster lamented the state of India's infrastructure and population management, feeling that the country is doomed if it does not improve.

Some respondents discussed the potential for India to become a global economic superpower, but noted that this would require leaders with more courage and a military capable of invasions, not just deterrence.

One poster also questioned whether India is infiltrated and backed by foreign powers to remain unstable and prevent actual development.

The thread also included criticism of current politicians, with one poster stating that they are "sovlless" and would sell out for personal gain.

The discussion concluded with various perspectives on India's development, military capabilities, and social issues, but no clear solutions or consensus emerged.

articles.title: Anon Praises 'Animal', Criticizes Bollywood's Caste Portrayal
articles.body:
Anon expressed admiration for the movie 'Animal', calling it a 'fantastic movie' with a 'very good story on toxic father situations.' They enjoyed it more than 'Dhurandhar' and appreciated its 'literally me moments', such as a scene with a swastika.

Another anon responded, criticizing the 'midwits' who dislike popular movies, stating that hating simple things is a common trait of people who think they are more intelligent. This anon also mentioned that while they enjoyed 'Animal', they wished the execution was better.

A different anon brought up the topic of caste, discussing the portrayal of low-caste individuals in movies.

They claimed that low-caste people are often depicted as harmless, but in reality, they are 'like a horde of roaches.' This anon also mentioned that even low-caste individuals do not trust their own children around other low-castes.

One respondent criticized the repetitive discussion of low-caste hate, suggesting that it would be better to contain such threads and focus on specific cases rather than general statements. Another anon agreed, stating that they appreciate organic discussions but find repetitive posts tiresome.

The conversation also touched on the topic of Bollywood's portrayal of upper-caste individuals, with one anon stating that Bhanglywood (a derogatory term for Bollywood) has ruined the image of upper-castes in the eyes of urban people.

Another anon praised 'Animal' for its unique storytelling and entertainment value, saying it was 'pure fucking entertainment' without any political agenda.

The discussion also involved criticism of Ranbir Kapoor's acting and the movie's execution, with one anon stating that it could have been a masterpiece if done better.

articles.title: Anon Banned from Reddit for Threatening Bangladesh and Pakistan
articles.body:
Anon was banned from Reddit after posting that they would 'nuke Bangladesh and cut Pakistan into pieces' in a thread on r/indianteenagers.

The OP claimed they were banned by 'parasitic pricks' and referred to Indian teenagers on the platform as 'a bunch of jihadies running all Indian Subreddits.' One respondent suggested that the OP's ban might be related to the presence of 'librandus' from Dalli operating Indian subreddits.

Another claimed that 'lineararray,' a Reddit user, runs most Indian subreddits and is 'a genuine tranny.' The conversation turned to the topic of Shudra conquest and the relationships between different groups.

Anon stated that Shudras are 'conquering' other groups, while another respondent mentioned 'dalit-jeets in USA' getting involved with white women.

Some posters expressed anti-librandu sentiments, with one stating that 'librandus' have been 'groomed' by mods and are responsible for problems on Indian subreddits.

The discussion also touched on the idea of 'genetic diversity' and 'breeding timmy putris.' The tone of the conversation was often hostile and divisive, with posters using derogatory language to refer to various groups.

One respondent approved of 'total tranny mod death,' while another stated that 'the less subhumans on planet, the better for everyone.'

articles.title: Anon compares subsaharan africa to India
articles.body:
Anon expressed fear that people from subsaharan africa could flood India. "They have 0 impulse control like a homo erectus," they wrote.

Another anon responded, saying India is objectively better than subsaharan africa, citing its nuclear capabilities and space program.

A respondent claimed that people from subsaharan africa are "not even human beings" and that "one third of their geno is literally non sapien." Anon also referenced Candace Owens, suggesting she could be used for "mating press position." Some users hurled insults, with one saying "Defending niggers is genuinely some of the lowest of low caste subhuman roach hill to die on."

articles.title: Anon Speculates on Caste of Fellow Posters
articles.body:
Anon opened the discussion by suggesting that if posters' castes were visible, certain patterns would emerge. They claimed that 'gorimem posts' would be most common, followed by OBC, high-IQ academic discussions from Brahmins, and so on.

One respondent joked about being a Brahmin who supports OBC, making a tongue-in-cheek comment about making the USA 'OBC again.' Another poster chimed in, describing a poster who writes lengthy essays on evolution and biology as likely being from a specific background.

A self-described 'poohadi' poster chimed in, denying certain claims and stating they are not representative of all poohadis. The conversation involved speculation about the caste backgrounds of various posters and their writing styles.
